.

, in boxes 14–17 on your answer sheet.

obj.title: The global importance of wetlands
obj.content:
[br/][p:a]Wetlands are areas where water covers the soil, or is present either at or near the surface of the soil, for all or part of the year. These are complex ecosystems, rich in unique plant and animal life. But according to the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWFN), half of the world's wetlands have disappeared since 1990 – converted or destroyed for commercial development, drainage schemes and the extraction of minerals and peat*. Many of those that remain have been damaged by agricultural pesticides and fertilisers, industrial pollutants, and construction works.[br/][br/]
[p:b]Throughout history, humans have gathered around wetlands, and their fertile ecosystems have played an important part in human development. Consequently, they are of considerable religious, historical and archaeological value to many communities around the world. 'Wetlands directly support the livelihoods and well-being of millions of people,'says Dr Matthew McCartney, principal researcher and hydrologist at the International Water Management Institute (IWMI). 'In many developing countries, large numbers of people are dependent on wetland agriculture for their livelihoods.'[br/][br/]
[p:c]They also serve a crucial environmental purpose. 'Wetlands are one of the key tools in mitigating climate change across the planet,' says Pieter van Eijk, head of Climate Adaptation at Wetlands International (WI), pointing to their use as buffers that protect coastal areas from sea-level rise and extreme weather events such as hurricanes and flooding. Wetland coastal forests provide food and water, as well as shelter from storms, and WI and other agencies are working to restore those forests which have been lost. 'It can be as simple as planting a few trees per hectare to create shade and substantially change a microclimate,' he says.'Implementing climate change projects isn't so much about money.'[br/][br/]
[p:d]The world's wetlands are, unfortunately, rich sources for in-demand commodities, such as palm oil and pulpwood. Peatlands – wetlands with a waterlogged organic soil layer – are particularly targeted. When peatlands are drained for cultivation, they become net carbon emitters instead of active carbon stores, and, according to Marcel Silvius, head of Climate- smart Land-use at WI, this practice causes six per cent of all global carbon emissions. The clearance of peatlands for planting also increases the risk of forest fires, which release huge amounts of CO2. 'We're seeing huge peatland forests with extremely high biodiversity value being lost for a few decades of oil palm revenues,' says Silvius.[br/][br/]
[p:e]The damage starts when logging companies arrive to clear the trees. They dig ditches to enter the peat swamps by boat and then float the logs out the same way. These are then used to drain water out of the peatlands to allow for the planting of corn, oil palms or pulpwood trees. Once the water has drained away, bacteria and fungi then break down the carbon in the peat and turn it into CO2 and methane. Meanwhile, the remainder of the solid matter in the peat starts to move downwards, in a process known as subsidence**. Peat comprises 90 per cent water, so this is one of the most alarming consequences of peatland clearances. 'In the tropics, peat subsides at about four centimetres a year, so within half a century, very large landscapes on Sumatra and Borneo will become flooded as the peat drops below water level,' says Silvius. 'It's a huge catastrophe that's in preparation. Some provinces will lose 40 per cent of their landmass.'[br/][br/]
[p:f]And while these industries affect wetlands in ways that can easily be documented, Dr Dave Tickner of the WWFN believes that more subtle impacts can be even more devastating. 'Sediment run-off and fertilisers can be pretty invisible,' says Tickner.'Over-extraction of water is equally invisible. You do get shock stories about rivers running red, or even catching fire, but there's seldom one big impact that really hurts a wetland.' Tickner does not blame anyone for deliberate damage, however. 'I've worked on wetland issues for 20 years and have never met anybody who wanted to damage a wetland,' he says. 'It isn't something that people generally set out to do. Quite often, the effects simply come from people trying to make a living.'[br/][br/]
[p:g]Silvius also acknowledges the importance of income generation. 'It's not that we just want to restore the biodiversity of wetlands – which we do – but we recognise there's a need to provide an income for local people.' This approach is supported by IWMI. 'The idea is that people in a developing country will only protect wetlands if they value and profit from them,' says McCartney. 'For sustainability, it's essential that local people are involved in wetland planning and decision making and have clear rights to use wetlands.'[br/][br/]
[p:h]The fortunes of wetlands would be improved, Silvius suggests, if more governments recognised their long-term value. 'Different governments have different attitudes,' he says, and goes on to explain that some countries place a high priority on restoring wetlands, while others still deny the issue. McCartney is cautiously optimistic, however. 'Awareness of the importance of wetlands is growing,' he says. 'It's true that wetland degradation still continues at a rapid pace, but my impression is that things are slowly changing.'[br/][br/]
* peat: a brown deposit formed by the partial decomposition of vegetation in wet acidic conditions, often cut out and dried for use as fuel[br/][br/]
** subsidence: the sinking of the Earth's surface, most often caused by the removal of resources from the ground
obj.content_zh:
[br/][b]A.[/b]湿地是指全年或部分时间水覆盖土壤，或在土壤表面或接近土壤表面的地区。这些是复杂的生态系统，富含独特的植物和动物。但是根据世界自然基金会(WWFN)的数据，自1990年以来，世界上一半的湿地已经消失，它们被用于商业开发、排水系统以及开采矿物和泥炭而被改造或破坏。许多幸存下来的建筑已经被农业农药和化肥、工业污染物和建筑工程破坏。[br/][br/]
[b]B.[/b]纵观历史，人类一直聚集在湿地周围，湿地肥沃的生态系统在人类发展中发挥了重要作用。因此，它们对世界各地的许多社区具有相当大的宗教、历史和考古价值。“湿地直接支持着数百万人的生计和福祉，”国际水管理研究所(IWMI)首席研究员兼水文学家马修·麦卡特尼博士说。“在许多发展中国家，大量人口依靠湿地农业为生。”[br/][br/]
[b]C.[/b]它们还有一个重要的环保目的。“湿地是减缓全球气候变化的关键工具之一，”湿地国际组织(Wetlands International, WI)气候适应项目负责人彼得·范·艾克(Pieter van Eijk)说。他指出，湿地可以作为缓冲，保护沿海地区免受海平面上升和飓风、洪水等极端天气事件的影响。沿海湿地森林提供食物和水，以及躲避风暴的庇护所，世界自然基金会和其他机构正在努力恢复这些失去的森林。他说:“这可以很简单，比如每公顷种植几棵树来创造树荫，并大幅改变小气候。”实施气候变化项目与资金关系不大。[br/][br/]
[b]D.[/b]不幸的是，世界上的湿地是棕榈油和纸浆木等需求商品的丰富来源。泥炭地——具有浸水有机土层的湿地——是特别有针对性的。当泥炭地被抽干用于耕作时，它们就变成了净碳排放者，而不是活跃的碳储存库。根据WI气候智慧型土地利用项目负责人Marcel Silvius的说法，这种做法导致了全球6%的碳排放。清除泥炭地用于种植也增加了森林火灾的风险，从而释放出大量的二氧化碳。西尔维乌斯说:“我们看到具有极高生物多样性价值的巨大泥炭地森林在几十年的油棕收入中消失了。”[br/][br/]
[b]E.[/b]当伐木公司来砍伐树木时，破坏就开始了。他们挖沟渠，乘船进入泥炭沼泽，然后用同样的方式把原木漂出去。然后，这些泥炭地用来排水，以便种植玉米、油棕或纸浆树。一旦水被排干，细菌和真菌就会分解泥炭中的碳，将其转化为二氧化碳和甲烷。与此同时，泥炭中剩余的固体物质开始向下移动，这一过程被称为沉降。泥炭含有90%的水，因此这是清除泥炭地最令人担忧的后果之一。西尔维乌斯说:“在热带地区，泥炭每年下沉约4厘米，所以在半个世纪内，随着泥炭下降到水位以下，苏门答腊岛和婆罗洲的大片土地将被淹没。”这是一场正在酝酿中的巨大灾难。一些省份将失去40%的陆地面积。”[br/][br/]
[b]F.[/b]虽然这些工业对湿地的影响可以很容易地记录下来，但世界自然基金会的戴夫·蒂克纳博士认为，更微妙的影响可能更具破坏性。蒂克纳说:“沉积物流失和肥料几乎是看不见的。”过度开采水资源同样是看不见的。你确实会听到一些令人震惊的故事，比如河流变红，甚至着火，但很少有什么重大影响真正损害了湿地。”然而，Tickner并没有指责任何人故意破坏。他说，我在湿地问题上工作了20年，从未遇到过任何想破坏湿地的人。“这不是人们通常打算做的事情。很多时候，这些影响仅仅来自于那些试图谋生的人。”[br/][br/]
[b]G.[/b]西尔维乌斯也承认创收的重要性。“我们不仅仅是想恢复湿地的生物多样性——我们确实这么做了——但我们认识到有必要为当地人提供收入。”IWMI支持这种方法。麦卡特尼说:“这个想法是，发展中国家的人们只有在重视湿地并从中获利的情况下才会保护它们。”“为了可持续发展，当地居民必须参与湿地规划和决策，并拥有使用湿地的明确权利。”[br/][br/]
[b]H.[/b]西尔维乌斯认为，如果更多的政府认识到湿地的长期价值，湿地的命运将得到改善。他说，不同的政府有不同的态度。他接着解释说，一些国家高度重视恢复湿地，而另一些国家仍然否认这个问题。然而，麦卡特尼对此持谨慎乐观态度。他说，人们越来越意识到湿地的重要性。“的确，湿地退化仍在快速进行，但我的印象是，情况正在慢慢改变。”[br/][br/]
泥炭:在潮湿的酸性条件下，由于植被部分分解而形成的棕色沉积物，通常被切下来晾干用作燃料[br/][br/]
沉降:地球表面的下沉，通常是由于地下资源的流失造成的
obj.html_content:
aWetlands are areas where water covers the soil, or is present either at or near the surface of the soil, for all or part of the year. These are complex ecosystems, rich in unique plant and animal life. But according to the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWFN), half of the world's wetlands have disappeared since 1990 – converted or destroyed for commercial development, drainage schemes and the extraction of minerals and peat*. Many of those that remain have been damaged by agricultural pesticides and fertilisers, industrial pollutants, and construction works.



bThroughout history, humans have gathered around wetlands, and their fertile ecosystems have played an important part in human development. Consequently, they are of considerable religious, historical and archaeological value to many communities around the world. 'Wetlands directly support the livelihoods and well-being of millions of people,'says Dr Matthew McCartney, principal researcher and hydrologist at the International Water Management Institute (IWMI). 'In many developing countries, large numbers of people are dependent on wetland agriculture for their livelihoods.'



cThey also serve a crucial environmental purpose. 'Wetlands are one of the key tools in mitigating climate change across the planet,' says Pieter van Eijk, head of Climate Adaptation at Wetlands International (WI), pointing to their use as buffers that protect coastal areas from sea-level rise and extreme weather events such as hurricanes and flooding. Wetland coastal forests provide food and water, as well as shelter from storms, and WI and other agencies are working to restore those forests which have been lost. 'It can be as simple as planting a few trees per hectare to create shade and substantially change a microclimate,' he says.'Implementing climate change projects isn't so much about money.'



dThe world's wetlands are, unfortunately, rich sources for in-demand commodities, such as palm oil and pulpwood. Peatlands – wetlands with a waterlogged organic soil layer – are particularly targeted. When peatlands are drained for cultivation, they become net carbon emitters instead of active carbon stores, and, according to Marcel Silvius, head of Climate- smart Land-use at WI, this practice causes six per cent of all global carbon emissions. The clearance of peatlands for planting also increases the risk of forest fires, which release huge amounts of CO2. 'We're seeing huge peatland forests with extremely high biodiversity value being lost for a few decades of oil palm revenues,' says Silvius.



eThe damage starts when logging companies arrive to clear the trees. They dig ditches to enter the peat swamps by boat and then float the logs out the same way. These are then used to drain water out of the peatlands to allow for the planting of corn, oil palms or pulpwood trees. Once the water has drained away, bacteria and fungi then break down the carbon in the peat and turn it into CO2 and methane. Meanwhile, the remainder of the solid matter in the peat starts to move downwards, in a process known as subsidence**. Peat comprises 90 per cent water, so this is one of the most alarming consequences of peatland clearances. 'In the tropics, peat subsides at about four centimetres a year, so within half a century, very large landscapes on Sumatra and Borneo will become flooded as the peat drops below water level,' says Silvius. 'It's a huge catastrophe that's in preparation. Some provinces will lose 40 per cent of their landmass.'



fAnd while these industries affect wetlands in ways that can easily be documented, Dr Dave Tickner of the WWFN believes that more subtle impacts can be even more devastating. 'Sediment run-off and fertilisers can be pretty invisible,' says Tickner.'Over-extraction of water is equally invisible. You do get shock stories about rivers running red, or even catching fire, but there's seldom one big impact that really hurts a wetland.' Tickner does not blame anyone for deliberate damage, however. 'I've worked on wetland issues for 20 years and have never met anybody who wanted to damage a wetland,' he says. 'It isn't something that people generally set out to do. Quite often, the effects simply come from people trying to make a living.'



gSilvius also acknowledges the importance of income generation. 'It's not that we just want to restore the biodiversity of wetlands – which we do – but we recognise there's a need to provide an income for local people.' This approach is supported by IWMI. 'The idea is that people in a developing country will only protect wetlands if they value and profit from them,' says McCartney. 'For sustainability, it's essential that local people are involved in wetland planning and decision making and have clear rights to use wetlands.'



hThe fortunes of wetlands would be improved, Silvius suggests, if more governments recognised their long-term value. 'Different governments have different attitudes,' he says, and goes on to explain that some countries place a high priority on restoring wetlands, while others still deny the issue. McCartney is cautiously optimistic, however. 'Awareness of the importance of wetlands is growing,' he says. 'It's true that wetland degradation still continues at a rapid pace, but my impression is that things are slowly changing.'



* peat: a brown deposit formed by the partial decomposition of vegetation in wet acidic conditions, often cut out and dried for use as fuel



** subsidence: the sinking of the Earth's surface, most often caused by the removal of resources from the ground

obj.content:
Peatlands which have been drained begin to release [blank]18[/blank] instead of storing it.[br/]
Once peatland areas have been cleared, [blank]19[/blank] are more likely to occur.[br/]
Clearing peatland forests to make way for oil palm plantations destroys the [blank]20[/blank] of the local environment.[br/]
Water is drained out of peatlands through the [blank]21[/blank] which are created by logging companies.[br/]
Draining peatlands leads to  [blank]22[/blank]: a serious problem which can eventually result in coastal flooding and land loss.
obj.html_content:
Peatlands which have been drained begin to release instead of storing it.
Once peatland areas have been cleared, are more likely to occur.
Clearing peatland forests to make way for oil palm plantations destroys the of the local environment.
Water is drained out of peatlands through the which are created by logging companies.
Draining peatlands leads to : a serious problem which can eventually result in coastal flooding and land loss.

added_content.ex_information:
Complete the sentences below.
Choose
ONE WORD ONLY
from the passage for each answer.
Write your answers in boxes 18–22 on your answer sheet.

obj.title: The global importance of wetlands

[br/][b]A.[/b]湿地是指全年或部分时间水覆盖土壤，或在土壤表面或接近土壤表面的地区。这些是复杂的生态系统，富含独特的植物和动物。但是根据世界自然基金会(WWFN)的数据，自1990年以来，世界上一半的湿地已经消失，它们被用于商业开发、排水系统以及开采矿物和泥炭而被改造或破坏。许多幸存下来的建筑已经被农业农药和化肥、工业污染物和建筑工程破坏。[br/][br/]

[b]C.[/b]它们还有一个重要的环保目的。“湿地是减缓全球气候变化的关键工具之一，”湿地国际组织(Wetlands International, WI)气候适应项目负责人彼得·范·艾克(Pieter van Eijk)说。他指出，湿地可以作为缓冲，保护沿海地区免受海平面上升和飓风、洪水等极端天气事件的影响。沿海湿地森林提供食物和水，以及躲避风暴的庇护所，世界自然基金会和其他机构正在努力恢复这些失去的森林。他说:“这可以很简单，比如每公顷种植几棵树来创造树荫，并大幅改变小气候。”实施气候变化项目与资金关系不大。[br/][br/]
[b]D.[/b]不幸的是，世界上的湿地是棕榈油和纸浆木等需求商品的丰富来源。泥炭地——具有浸水有机土层的湿地——是特别有针对性的。当泥炭地被抽干用于耕作时，它们就变成了净碳排放者，而不是活跃的碳储存库。根据WI气候智慧型土地利用项目负责人Marcel Silvius的说法，这种做法导致了全球6%的碳排放。清除泥炭地用于种植也增加了森林火灾的风险，从而释放出大量的二氧化碳。西尔维乌斯说:“我们看到具有极高生物多样性价值的巨大泥炭地森林在几十年的油棕收入中消失了。”[br/][br/]
[b]E.[/b]当伐木公司来砍伐树木时，破坏就开始了。他们挖沟渠，乘船进入泥炭沼泽，然后用同样的方式把原木漂出去。然后，这些泥炭地用来排水，以便种植玉米、油棕或纸浆树。一旦水被排干，细菌和真菌就会分解泥炭中的碳，将其转化为二氧化碳和甲烷。与此同时，泥炭中剩余的固体物质开始向下移动，这一过程被称为沉降。泥炭含有90%的水，因此这是清除泥炭地最令人担忧的后果之一。西尔维乌斯说:“在热带地区，泥炭每年下沉约4厘米，所以在半个世纪内，随着泥炭下降到水位以下，苏门答腊岛和婆罗洲的大片土地将被淹没。”这是一场正在酝酿中的巨大灾难。一些省份将失去40%的陆地面积。”[br/][br/]
[b]F.[/b]虽然这些工业对湿地的影响可以很容易地记录下来，但世界自然基金会的戴夫·蒂克纳博士认为，更微妙的影响可能更具破坏性。蒂克纳说:“沉积物流失和肥料几乎是看不见的。”过度开采水资源同样是看不见的。你确实会听到一些令人震惊的故事，比如河流变红，甚至着火，但很少有什么重大影响真正损害了湿地。”然而，Tickner并没有指责任何人故意破坏。他说，我在湿地问题上工作了20年，从未遇到过任何想破坏湿地的人。“这不是人们通常打算做的事情。很多时候，这些影响仅仅来自于那些试图谋生的人。”[br/][br/]
[b]G.[/b]西尔维乌斯也承认创收的重要性。“我们不仅仅是想恢复湿地的生物多样性——我们确实这么做了——但我们认识到有必要为当地人提供收入。”IWMI支持这种方法。麦卡特尼说:“这个想法是，发展中国家的人们只有在重视湿地并从中获利的情况下才会保护它们。”“为了可持续发展，当地居民必须参与湿地规划和决策，并拥有使用湿地的明确权利。”[br/][br/]
[b]H.[/b]西尔维乌斯认为，如果更多的政府认识到湿地的长期价值，湿地的命运将得到改善。他说，不同的政府有不同的态度。他接着解释说，一些国家高度重视恢复湿地，而另一些国家仍然否认这个问题。然而，麦卡特尼对此持谨慎乐观态度。他说，人们越来越意识到湿地的重要性。“的确，湿地退化仍在快速进行，但我的印象是，情况正在慢慢改变。”[br/][br/]
泥炭:在潮湿的酸性条件下，由于植被部分分解而形成的棕色沉积物，通常被切下来晾干用作燃料[br/][br/]
沉降:地球表面的下沉，通常是由于地下资源的流失造成的
obj.html_content:
aWetlands are areas where water covers the soil, or is present either at or near the surface of the soil, for all or part of the year. These are complex ecosystems, rich in unique plant and animal life. But according to the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWFN), half of the world's wetlands have disappeared since 1990 – converted or destroyed for commercial development, drainage schemes and the extraction of minerals and peat*. Many of those that remain have been damaged by agricultural pesticides and fertilisers, industrial pollutants, and construction works.



bThroughout history, humans have gathered around wetlands, and their fertile ecosystems have played an important part in human development. Consequently, they are of considerable religious, historical and archaeological value to many communities around the world. 'Wetlands directly support the livelihoods and well-being of millions of people,'says Dr Matthew McCartney, principal researcher and hydrologist at the International Water Management Institute (IWMI). 'In many developing countries, large numbers of people are dependent on wetland agriculture for their livelihoods.'



cThey also serve a crucial environmental purpose. 'Wetlands are one of the key tools in mitigating climate change across the planet,' says Pieter van Eijk, head of Climate Adaptation at Wetlands International (WI), pointing to their use as buffers that protect coastal areas from sea-level rise and extreme weather events such as hurricanes and flooding. Wetland coastal forests provide food and water, as well as shelter from storms, and WI and other agencies are working to restore those forests which have been lost. 'It can be as simple as planting a few trees per hectare to create shade and substantially change a microclimate,' he says.'Implementing climate change projects isn't so much about money.'



dThe world's wetlands are, unfortunately, rich sources for in-demand commodities, such as palm oil and pulpwood. Peatlands – wetlands with a waterlogged organic soil layer – are particularly targeted. When peatlands are drained for cultivation, they become net carbon emitters instead of active carbon stores, and, according to Marcel Silvius, head of Climate- smart Land-use at WI, this practice causes six per cent of all global carbon emissions. The clearance of peatlands for planting also increases the risk of forest fires, which release huge amounts of CO2. 'We're seeing huge peatland forests with extremely high biodiversity value being lost for a few decades of oil palm revenues,' says Silvius.



eThe damage starts when logging companies arrive to clear the trees. They dig ditches to enter the peat swamps by boat and then float the logs out the same way. These are then used to drain water out of the peatlands to allow for the planting of corn, oil palms or pulpwood trees. Once the water has drained away, bacteria and fungi then break down the carbon in the peat and turn it into CO2 and methane. Meanwhile, the remainder of the solid matter in the peat starts to move downwards, in a process known as subsidence**. Peat comprises 90 per cent water, so this is one of the most alarming consequences of peatland clearances. 'In the tropics, peat subsides at about four centimetres a year, so within half a century, very large landscapes on Sumatra and Borneo will become flooded as the peat drops below water level,' says Silvius. 'It's a huge catastrophe that's in preparation. Some provinces will lose 40 per cent of their landmass.'



fAnd while these industries affect wetlands in ways that can easily be documented, Dr Dave Tickner of the WWFN believes that more subtle impacts can be even more devastating. 'Sediment run-off and fertilisers can be pretty invisible,' says Tickner.'Over-extraction of water is equally invisible. You do get shock stories about rivers running red, or even catching fire, but there's seldom one big impact that really hurts a wetland.' Tickner does not blame anyone for deliberate damage, however. 'I've worked on wetland issues for 20 years and have never met anybody who wanted to damage a wetland,' he says. 'It isn't something that people generally set out to do. Quite often, the effects simply come from people trying to make a living.'



gSilvius also acknowledges the importance of income generation. 'It's not that we just want to restore the biodiversity of wetlands – which we do – but we recognise there's a need to provide an income for local people.' This approach is supported by IWMI. 'The idea is that people in a developing country will only protect wetlands if they value and profit from them,' says McCartney. 'For sustainability, it's essential that local people are involved in wetland planning and decision making and have clear rights to use wetlands.'



hThe fortunes of wetlands would be improved, Silvius suggests, if more governments recognised their long-term value. 'Different governments have different attitudes,' he says, and goes on to explain that some countries place a high priority on restoring wetlands, while others still deny the issue. McCartney is cautiously optimistic, however. 'Awareness of the importance of wetlands is growing,' he says. 'It's true that wetland degradation still continues at a rapid pace, but my impression is that things are slowly changing.'



* peat: a brown deposit formed by the partial decomposition of vegetation in wet acidic conditions, often cut out and dried for use as fuel



** subsidence: the sinking of the Earth's surface, most often caused by the removal of resources from the ground

added_content.ex_information:
Look at the following statements (Questions 23–26) and the list of experts below.
Match each statement with the correct expert,
A–D
.
Write the correct letter,
A–D
, in boxes 23–26 on your answer sheet.

obj.content: Communities living in wetland regions must be included in discussions about the future of these areas.[match]23[/match]
obj.html_content: Communities living in wetland regions must be included in discussions about the future of these areas.23

obj.content: Official policies towards wetlands vary from one nation to the next.[match]24[/match]
obj.html_content: Official policies towards wetlands vary from one nation to the next.24

obj.content: People cause harm to wetlands without having any intention to do so.[match]25[/match]
obj.html_content: People cause harm to wetlands without having any intention to do so.25

obj.content: Initiatives to reverse environmental damage need not be complex.[match]26[/match]
obj.html_content: Initiatives to reverse environmental damage need not be complex.26

obj.title: The global importance of wetlands
obj.content:
[br/][p:a]Wetlands are areas where water covers the soil, or is present either at or near the surface of the soil, for all or part of the year. These are complex ecosystems, rich in unique plant and animal life. But according to the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWFN), half of the world's wetlands have disappeared since 1990 – converted or destroyed for commercial development, drainage schemes and the extraction of minerals and peat*. Many of those that remain have been damaged by agricultural pesticides and fertilisers, industrial pollutants, and construction works.[br/][br/]
[p:b]Throughout history, humans have gathered around wetlands, and their fertile ecosystems have played an important part in human development. Consequently, they are of considerable religious, historical and archaeological value to many communities around the world. 'Wetlands directly support the livelihoods and well-being of millions of people,'says Dr Matthew McCartney, principal researcher and hydrologist at the International Water Management Institute (IWMI). 'In many developing countries, large numbers of people are dependent on wetland agriculture for their livelihoods.'[br/][br/]
[p:c]They also serve a crucial environmental purpose. 'Wetlands are one of the key tools in mitigating climate change across the planet,' says Pieter van Eijk, head of Climate Adaptation at Wetlands International (WI), pointing to their use as buffers that protect coastal areas from sea-level rise and extreme weather events such as hurricanes and flooding. Wetland coastal forests provide food and water, as well as shelter from storms, and WI and other agencies are working to restore those forests which have been lost. 'It can be as simple as planting a few trees per hectare to create shade and substantially change a microclimate,' he says.'Implementing climate change projects isn't so much about money.'[br/][br/]
[p:d]The world's wetlands are, unfortunately, rich sources for in-demand commodities, such as palm oil and pulpwood. Peatlands – wetlands with a waterlogged organic soil layer – are particularly targeted. When peatlands are drained for cultivation, they become net carbon emitters instead of active carbon stores, and, according to Marcel Silvius, head of Climate- smart Land-use at WI, this practice causes six per cent of all global carbon emissions. The clearance of peatlands for planting also increases the risk of forest fires, which release huge amounts of CO2. 'We're seeing huge peatland forests with extremely high biodiversity value being lost for a few decades of oil palm revenues,' says Silvius.[br/][br/]
[p:e]The damage starts when logging companies arrive to clear the trees. They dig ditches to enter the peat swamps by boat and then float the logs out the same way. These are then used to drain water out of the peatlands to allow for the planting of corn, oil palms or pulpwood trees. Once the water has drained away, bacteria and fungi then break down the carbon in the peat and turn it into CO2 and methane. Meanwhile, the remainder of the solid matter in the peat starts to move downwards, in a process known as subsidence**. Peat comprises 90 per cent water, so this is one of the most alarming consequences of peatland clearances. 'In the tropics, peat subsides at about four centimetres a year, so within half a century, very large landscapes on Sumatra and Borneo will become flooded as the peat drops below water level,' says Silvius. 'It's a huge catastrophe that's in preparation. Some provinces will lose 40 per cent of their landmass.'[br/][br/]
[p:f]And while these industries affect wetlands in ways that can easily be documented, Dr Dave Tickner of the WWFN believes that more subtle impacts can be even more devastating. 'Sediment run-off and fertilisers can be pretty invisible,' says Tickner.'Over-extraction of water is equally invisible. You do get shock stories about rivers running red, or even catching fire, but there's seldom one big impact that really hurts a wetland.' Tickner does not blame anyone for deliberate damage, however. 'I've worked on wetland issues for 20 years and have never met anybody who wanted to damage a wetland,' he says. 'It isn't something that people generally set out to do. Quite often, the effects simply come from people trying to make a living.'[br/][br/]
[p:g]Silvius also acknowledges the importance of income generation. 'It's not that we just want to restore the biodiversity of wetlands – which we do – but we recognise there's a need to provide an income for local people.' This approach is supported by IWMI. 'The idea is that people in a developing country will only protect wetlands if they value and profit from them,' says McCartney. 'For sustainability, it's essential that local people are involved in wetland planning and decision making and have clear rights to use wetlands.'[br/][br/]
[p:h]The fortunes of wetlands would be improved, Silvius suggests, if more governments recognised their long-term value. 'Different governments have different attitudes,' he says, and goes on to explain that some countries place a high priority on restoring wetlands, while others still deny the issue. McCartney is cautiously optimistic, however. 'Awareness of the importance of wetlands is growing,' he says. 'It's true that wetland degradation still continues at a rapid pace, but my impression is that things are slowly changing.'[br/][br/]
* peat: a brown deposit formed by the partial decomposition of vegetation in wet acidic conditions, often cut out and dried for use as fuel[br/][br/]
** subsidence: the sinking of the Earth's surface, most often caused by the removal of resources from the ground
obj.content_zh:
[br/][b]A.[/b]湿地是指全年或部分时间水覆盖土壤，或在土壤表面或接近土壤表面的地区。这些是复杂的生态系统，富含独特的植物和动物。但是根据世界自然基金会(WWFN)的数据，自1990年以来，世界上一半的湿地已经消失，它们被用于商业开发、排水系统以及开采矿物和泥炭而被改造或破坏。许多幸存下来的建筑已经被农业农药和化肥、工业污染物和建筑工程破坏。[br/][br/]
[b]B.[/b]纵观历史，人类一直聚集在湿地周围，湿地肥沃的生态系统在人类发展中发挥了重要作用。因此，它们对世界各地的许多社区具有相当大的宗教、历史和考古价值。“湿地直接支持着数百万人的生计和福祉，”国际水管理研究所(IWMI)首席研究员兼水文学家马修·麦卡特尼博士说。“在许多发展中国家，大量人口依靠湿地农业为生。”[br/][br/]
[b]C.[/b]它们还有一个重要的环保目的。“湿地是减缓全球气候变化的关键工具之一，”湿地国际组织(Wetlands International, WI)气候适应项目负责人彼得·范·艾克(Pieter van Eijk)说。他指出，湿地可以作为缓冲，保护沿海地区免受海平面上升和飓风、洪水等极端天气事件的影响。沿海湿地森林提供食物和水，以及躲避风暴的庇护所，世界自然基金会和其他机构正在努力恢复这些失去的森林。他说:“这可以很简单，比如每公顷种植几棵树来创造树荫，并大幅改变小气候。”实施气候变化项目与资金关系不大。[br/][br/]
[b]D.[/b]不幸的是，世界上的湿地是棕榈油和纸浆木等需求商品的丰富来源。泥炭地——具有浸水有机土层的湿地——是特别有针对性的。当泥炭地被抽干用于耕作时，它们就变成了净碳排放者，而不是活跃的碳储存库。根据WI气候智慧型土地利用项目负责人Marcel Silvius的说法，这种做法导致了全球6%的碳排放。清除泥炭地用于种植也增加了森林火灾的风险，从而释放出大量的二氧化碳。西尔维乌斯说:“我们看到具有极高生物多样性价值的巨大泥炭地森林在几十年的油棕收入中消失了。”[br/][br/]
[b]E.[/b]当伐木公司来砍伐树木时，破坏就开始了。他们挖沟渠，乘船进入泥炭沼泽，然后用同样的方式把原木漂出去。然后，这些泥炭地用来排水，以便种植玉米、油棕或纸浆树。一旦水被排干，细菌和真菌就会分解泥炭中的碳，将其转化为二氧化碳和甲烷。与此同时，泥炭中剩余的固体物质开始向下移动，这一过程被称为沉降。泥炭含有90%的水，因此这是清除泥炭地最令人担忧的后果之一。西尔维乌斯说:“在热带地区，泥炭每年下沉约4厘米，所以在半个世纪内，随着泥炭下降到水位以下，苏门答腊岛和婆罗洲的大片土地将被淹没。”这是一场正在酝酿中的巨大灾难。一些省份将失去40%的陆地面积。”[br/][br/]
[b]F.[/b]虽然这些工业对湿地的影响可以很容易地记录下来，但世界自然基金会的戴夫·蒂克纳博士认为，更微妙的影响可能更具破坏性。蒂克纳说:“沉积物流失和肥料几乎是看不见的。”过度开采水资源同样是看不见的。你确实会听到一些令人震惊的故事，比如河流变红，甚至着火，但很少有什么重大影响真正损害了湿地。”然而，Tickner并没有指责任何人故意破坏。他说，我在湿地问题上工作了20年，从未遇到过任何想破坏湿地的人。“这不是人们通常打算做的事情。很多时候，这些影响仅仅来自于那些试图谋生的人。”[br/][br/]

[b]H.[/b]西尔维乌斯认为，如果更多的政府认识到湿地的长期价值，湿地的命运将得到改善。他说，不同的政府有不同的态度。他接着解释说，一些国家高度重视恢复湿地，而另一些国家仍然否认这个问题。然而，麦卡特尼对此持谨慎乐观态度。他说，人们越来越意识到湿地的重要性。“的确，湿地退化仍在快速进行，但我的印象是，情况正在慢慢改变。”[br/][br/]

Quite often, the effects simply come from people trying to make a living.'



gSilvius also acknowledges the importance of income generation. 'It's not that we just want to restore the biodiversity of wetlands – which we do – but we recognise there's a need to provide an income for local people.' This approach is supported by IWMI. 'The idea is that people in a developing country will only protect wetlands if they value and profit from them,' says McCartney. 'For sustainability, it's essential that local people are involved in wetland planning and decision making and have clear rights to use wetlands.'



hThe fortunes of wetlands would be improved, Silvius suggests, if more governments recognised their long-term value. 'Different governments have different attitudes,' he says, and goes on to explain that some countries place a high priority on restoring wetlands, while others still deny the issue. McCartney is cautiously optimistic, however. 'Awareness of the importance of wetlands is growing,' he says. 'It's true that wetland degradation still continues at a rapid pace, but my impression is that things are slowly changing.'



* peat: a brown deposit formed by the partial decomposition of vegetation in wet acidic conditions, often cut out and dried for use as fuel



** subsidence: the sinking of the Earth's surface, most often caused by the removal of resources from the ground

officialExplain.explain:
译文：提及了需要确保湿地地带的居民能持续从中获益

如前文中提醒过的那样，在“段落匹配信息”这种类型的题目中，经常会有诸如reference、mention、account这类词汇出现，它们表达的意思都是“提到，说到”，属于对定位没有任何帮助的“废话词汇”，可以忽略不看。特别是reference一词，在其他语境中还可以有“参考”和“参考书目”的含义，但在这种匹配题中却并不表达这个意思。

本题除了定位靠后外，其实难度并不高。G段第二句话说“也需要为当地人口提供收入”，第四句话说“人们只有确实从湿地中受益才会想要去保护它们”，第五句话说“当地人需要有使用湿地的明确权力”，其实都是在表达类似于题干的意思，对应本题的出处段落：G。

译文：已经损失掉的湿地面积比例

本题出处非常靠前，来自原文A段的第三句话，而且用非常直白的half一词同义替换了题干中同样基础的认知词汇proportion。考生只要对这两个单词没有认知障碍，且耐心看懂了原文句中湿地受到的待遇converted or destroyed就相当于题干所说的been lost，就能比较轻松地锁定本题出处段落：A。

译文：提到这样一个看法，人们已经开始欣赏湿地的价值

本题除了出处靠后外，解题难度其实很低。无论是题干这句话，还是对应在全文中的最后两句话，从词汇到句型都非常简单易懂，考生只要能耐心看到全文结尾，明白这两句话说的是“人们越来越意识到湿地的重要性；虽然湿地仍在退化但尚有转机”，对应的正是题干内容，就能顺利确认本题出处段落：H。

译文：提到了湿地的文化重要性

本题的定位也比较靠前，位于B段。对于不熟悉雅思阅读中各种同义替换方式的考生来说，原文中第二句说湿地有着“religious, historical and archaeological value”，其中的三个形容词无论哪一个孤立来看似乎都不能严格对应题干中的cultural一词。但是如果考生采用了平行阅读方法来依次阅读文意，则更有可能结合上一句所说的“湿地自古以来就在人类发展中扮演着至关重要的作用”，理解到第二句话所描述的这些价值其实都是在委婉地表述并非从“柴米油盐”而是从“精神文化”这个方面对人类举足轻重，从而确认本题的出处段落为B。

译文：被抽干了水的泥炭地会开始释放______而非储存它。

本题有一模一样的题干词peatlands出现在D段第二句话里，虽然这句话没有给出解题相关信息，但紧接着的下一句中就跟进出现了另一个题干原词drained。考生只需看懂这句话里的they become net carbon emitters instead of active carbon stores，就应当能根据emitters与题干release在词义上的对应，顺利确认本题答案应当为释放的对象“碳”。退一步说，即使不熟悉emitters这个词，应当也可以通过句中instead of这个前后转折对比，排除active carbon stores这个部分的内容，顺利得出答案应为carbon。

译文：一旦泥炭地被清理，______就更容易发生。

本题出处紧随上一题，且题干中的动词cleared在原文中仅仅是换成了名词形式的clearance，很好定位。此外，原文中increases the risk of和题干中more likely to occur之间的对应，无论从词汇认知难度还是表述理解难度来看都非常简单，考生应当能顺利得出本题答案：fires。

译文：砍伐泥炭地森林来让位于油棕榈种植的做法破坏了当地环境的______。

本题定位也是紧跟上一题，且包含一模一样的题干信息oil palm，考生只需要看懂原文中说being lost的对象是extremely high biodiversity value“极高的生物多样性价值”，应该就能顺利锁定本题的正确答案为biodiversity。

译文：水通过伐木公司制造出来的______被排出泥炭地。

本题可以通过题干中的logging companies定位到E段开头第一句。虽然这句话本身只是出现了“伐木公司”而没有给出进一步信息，但考生只需耐心再向后读一句，就能大致看懂这些公司是挖沟（dig ditches）以乘船进入泥炭地森林，再靠这条路径把伐好的原木运出来，即使不认识“沟渠”这个词，也不难推理出答案应为ditches。

译文：将泥炭地排水会导致______：这是一个严重的问题，最终有可能会造成海岸地区洪水和土地面积损失。

此题如果想只靠题干关键词进行快速准确定位，恐怕会遭遇一些困难。其实在E段的第五句跟进讲述draining peatlands都有哪些害处时，就已经出现了“沉降”这个正确答案了。然而，一来这个单词本身比较生僻，虽然原文很贴心地在底部给出了词义解释，但恐怕大多数时间紧张的考生都不会“浪费”宝贵时间去查看；二来，第五句话只是给出了这个答案词本身，至于“沉降”会造成哪些后果，也就是题干里说到的“海岸地区发洪水”和“陆地面积受损失”，其实来自后文的E段最后两句话。于是，还是在利用平行阅读法通读了全段文意之后，考生才会有更大把握确认本题答案应为subsidence。

译文：居住在湿地区域的社群必须参与事关这些地带未来的讨论。

本题的难度主要来自出题位置靠后，虽然这位Matthew McCartney在B段就出现过，然而对应他看法的真正解题内容却来自G段最后一句。不过考生只要看到了这里，看出题干communities与原文local people的对应，以及题干included in discussions与原文involved in wetland planning and decision making的对应，就不难看出本题最合适的选项为A。

译文：对待湿地的官方政策每个国家各有不同。

本题与上一道题类似，也是对应人名早在D段就出现过，但实际对应题干答案的原文却出现在H段；然而一旦考生读到了这里，就能轻而易举地从H段第二句里的Different governments have different attitudes看出这正是在对应题干中的vary from one nation to the next，从而笃定本题答案应为C。

译文：人们给湿地带来伤害时其实并非有意要这样做。

本题对应的这四句话，语法和词汇难度都很低，分别从不同维度表述了“人们并非有意去破坏湿地”。倒数第四句说Tickner并不责怪任何人蓄意破坏；倒数第三句说“他研究了20年也没见过任何人想要去破坏”；倒数第二句说“人们通常不会以破坏为目标”；最后一句说“人们只是想要谋生”。每一句话都“花式”对应了题干表述，考生无论利用哪句都能看出本题答案必定是D。

译文：扭转环境破坏的举措并不需要多么复杂。

本题是四道题中相对最简单的一道。首先，在文中的对应位置靠前，来自C段；其次，虽然人名出在第二句而实际答案出在最后两句，但对应句子简单直白，认真打好单词基础的考生应当可以顺利得出答案为B。
